displayName: Medical Malpractice Tribunal Offer of Proof (Massachusetts § 60B)
template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — MEDICAL MALPRACTICE TRIBUNAL OFFER OF PROOF (MA § 60B)**:
A written submission to the Massachusetts medical-malpractice tribunal at the outset of every action of malpractice, error, or mistake against a provider of health care, pursuant to **M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B**. The tribunal — composed of a single justice of the Superior Court, a member of the Massachusetts bar, and a physician licensed in the Commonwealth — convenes within fifteen (15) days after the defendant's answer and determines whether the evidence presented, if properly substantiated, is sufficient to raise a legitimate question of liability appropriate for judicial inquiry, or whether the plaintiff's case is merely an unfortunate medical result. If the tribunal finds the offer of proof insufficient, the plaintiff must post a bond of $6,000 to proceed; if not posted within thirty (30) days, the action is dismissed. Target length: 8–20 pages depending on case complexity.

**WHAT THIS TEMPLATE IS — AND WHAT IT IS NOT**:
This is a Massachusetts-specific tribunal offer of proof under M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B. It is NOT a federal certificate-of-merit affidavit (such as N.J.S.A. 2A:53A-27 Affidavit of Merit, IL 735 ILCS 5/2-622, GA O.C.G.A. § 9-11-9.1, or PA Pa.R.C.P. 1042.3) — those are filed at or shortly after the complaint and serve a similar gatekeeping purpose but operate procedurally distinctly. It is NOT the complaint itself (use `complaint_pi_ma`). It is NOT a motion to dismiss response. The tribunal's adverse finding does not preclude the plaintiff from proceeding (bond posts the case), and the offer is not admissible at trial — it serves a screening function only.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `defendantType`: `physician_individual` (default) | `hospital_or_facility` | `nursing_home` | `nurse_or_pa` | `dentist` | `psychiatrist_or_psychologist` | `other_health_care_provider`
- `claimType`: `failure_to_diagnose` | `delayed_diagnosis` | `surgical_error` | `medication_error` | `birth_injury` | `informed_consent_failure` | `nursing_negligence` | `discharge_negligence` | `other`
- `expertSpecialty`: required — the specialty of the proffered expert (must match defendant's specialty per **Bridgwood v. A.J. Wood Constr., Inc., 480 Mass. 349 (2018)** specialty-match guidance)
- `includeStandardOfCare`: boolean (default true)
- `includeBreach`: boolean (default true)
- `includeCausation`: boolean (default true)
- `includeDamages`: boolean (default true)
- `expertReportAttached`: boolean (default true) — the offer is typically supported by an attached expert affidavit/letter
- `includeRecordsCitations`: boolean (default true) — pinpoint citations to the medical-records foundation
- `includeBondNotice`: boolean (default true) — note plaintiff's intent to post bond if necessary
- `subPractice`: `med_mal` (default; this template is med-mal-specific) | `nursing_home_neglect` (sometimes filed under § 60B framework)

**STRUCTURE**:

### Caption Block
Standard Massachusetts Superior Court caption: `COMMONWEALTH OF MASSACHUSETTS / SUPERIOR COURT DEPARTMENT OF THE TRIAL COURT / [COUNTY], ss. / CIVIL ACTION NO. [number] / [Plaintiff name], / Plaintiff, / v. / [Defendant names], / Defendants. / **PLAINTIFF'S OFFER OF PROOF TO THE MEDICAL MALPRACTICE TRIBUNAL PURSUANT TO M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B**`.

### Introductory Paragraph
One paragraph: 'Pursuant to M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B, Plaintiff [name] respectfully submits this Offer of Proof to the Medical Malpractice Tribunal for the limited purpose of demonstrating that the evidence presented, if properly substantiated, is sufficient to raise a legitimate question of liability appropriate for judicial inquiry.'

### I. Standard for Tribunal Review
One paragraph: cite the controlling tribunal standard:
- **M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B** — tribunal determines whether the evidence is sufficient to raise a legitimate question of liability appropriate for judicial inquiry, OR whether the plaintiff's case is merely an unfortunate medical result for which the provider is not legally responsible.
- **Little v. Rosenthal, 376 Mass. 573, 578 (1978)** — at the tribunal stage, the offer is reviewed in the light most favorable to the plaintiff, and inferences are drawn in plaintiff's favor.
- **Cooper v. Cooper-Ciccarelli, 77 Mass. App. Ct. 86 (2010)** and **Kapp v. Ballantine, 380 Mass. 186 (1980)** — the offer must contain specific facts, supported by record evidence, that establish each element of the claim: (1) provider-patient relationship and standard of care, (2) breach, (3) causation, and (4) damages.
- **Bridgwood v. A.J. Wood Constr., Inc., 480 Mass. 349 (2018)** — the standard-of-care expert must possess training and experience in the same specialty (or substantially-similar specialty) as the defendant.

### II. The Patient and the Provider-Patient Relationship
One or two paragraphs identifying:
- The patient (plaintiff) — name, age at the time of treatment, presenting condition.
- The defendant provider(s) — name, specialty, role in the patient's care.
- The duration and scope of the provider-patient relationship — dates of treatment, facility (if applicable).
- The factual basis for the relationship and the duty of care that arose from it.

### III. Standard of Care [include only when includeStandardOfCare=true]
One or two paragraphs setting out the applicable standard of care, supported by the proffered expert (`expertSpecialty`). State:
- The standard of care applicable to a `[defendant_specialty]` treating a patient with `[claimType]` presentation.
- The expert's qualifications to opine on the standard (Bridgwood specialty-match requirement).
- Citation to medical-society guidelines, peer-reviewed literature, or specialty-society practice parameters where the expert relies on them.
- Express statement that the expert offers the opinion to a reasonable degree of medical certainty / probability.

### IV. Breach [include only when includeBreach=true]
One or two paragraphs setting out, with specific record support, where the defendant's care fell below the standard. Include `includeRecordsCitations` pinpoint cites to chart entries, test results, orders, medication-administration records, operative reports, or discharge summaries — by date and chart-page.
Adapt by `claimType`:
- `failure_to_diagnose` / `delayed_diagnosis`: the failure to order indicated work-up; the failure to recognize the differential diagnosis; the failure to follow up on abnormal findings; the temporal window of the missed diagnosis.
- `surgical_error`: the deviation from the surgical standard; identification of intra-operative landmarks; complications and the failure to recognize / address them.
- `medication_error`: the wrong medication, dose, route, frequency, or interval; the failure to recognize allergy, contraindication, or interaction.
- `birth_injury`: the failure to recognize fetal-distress patterns; failure to deliver timely; instrumentation deviations.
- `informed_consent_failure`: the failure to disclose material risks, alternatives, or anticipated outcomes.
- `nursing_negligence`: failure to monitor, escalate, or follow physician orders.
- `discharge_negligence`: premature discharge; failure to communicate post-discharge instructions; failure to arrange follow-up.

### V. Causation [include only when includeCausation=true]
One or two paragraphs setting out the causal link, supported by the expert opinion to a reasonable degree of medical certainty:
- The defendant's breach was a substantial contributing cause of the patient's injury, or in some cases, the loss of a substantial chance of a better outcome (under **Matsuyama v. Birnbaum, 452 Mass. 1 (2008)** loss-of-chance framework where applicable).
- The mechanism of injury — physiological pathway, temporal sequence, distinguishing the breach from any baseline disease progression.
- Apportionment between the breach and any pre-existing or coexisting condition.

### VI. Damages [include only when includeDamages=true]
One paragraph identifying the patient's resulting damages — physical injury, prolonged hospitalization, additional procedures, permanent impairment, mental anguish, lost earnings, lost earning capacity, loss-of-consortium claims (if any), and (in death cases) wrongful death and survival damages under **M.G.L. c. 229 § 2** and **§ 6**.

### VII. Conclusion
One paragraph: based on the foregoing, the offer of proof, viewed in the light most favorable to plaintiff, is sufficient to raise a legitimate question of liability appropriate for judicial inquiry. Plaintiff respectfully requests the tribunal so find. [If `includeBondNotice=true`] In the event the tribunal finds the offer insufficient, plaintiff intends to timely post the statutory bond pursuant to M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B and proceed to litigation on the merits.

### Counsel Signature Block
Standard counsel signature block.

### Certificate of Service
Standard certificate of service to defendant(s) and the tribunal coordinator.

**ATTACHMENTS** (typically appended):
- Expert affidavit / letter from `expertSpecialty` expert addressing standard of care, breach, and causation, with curriculum vitae attached.
- Pinpoint copies of the medical records cited in §§ III–V.
- Operative reports, imaging studies, lab results relied upon.
- Any prior expert reports obtained in pre-suit investigation.

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Do NOT submit a bare offer without the supporting expert affidavit. The tribunal will find a bare offer insufficient as a matter of course.
- Do NOT use a generic 'specialty' expert where Bridgwood requires specialty match — verify the expert's CV against the defendant's specialty before submission.
- Do NOT make legal arguments beyond the elements — the tribunal screens for evidentiary sufficiency, not legal merit. Lengthy argument is unhelpful and may invite a finding of insufficiency.
- Do NOT include settlement-evaluation or adversarial demand language — the tribunal is a screening body, and the offer is for screening purposes only.
- Do NOT cite cases for substantive legal propositions outside the tribunal-standard cases (Little, Kapp, Bridgwood, Matsuyama) and the medical-society / specialty-society guidelines — keep argument-and-authorities sections tight.
- Do NOT identify witnesses or experts not actually retained or available — the offer must be supportable.
- Do NOT propound discovery questions through the offer — discovery proceeds separately under Mass. R. Civ. P. 26 et seq.
- For any disabled `documentOptions` value (e.g., `includeDamages=false`), suppress the corresponding section entirely.
- Total document length: 8–20 pages plus attachments.
hints: Massachusetts Medical Malpractice Tribunal Offer of Proof under M.G.L. c. 231 § 60B. Tribunal (Superior Court Justice + MA bar member + MA physician) convenes within 15 days after answer; finding of insufficiency requires plaintiff to post $6,000 bond within 30 days or face dismissal. Cite Little v. Rosenthal (376 Mass. 573 (1978)) — light most favorable to plaintiff; Kapp v. Ballantine (380 Mass. 186 (1980)) and Cooper v. Cooper-Ciccarelli (77 Mass. App. Ct. 86 (2010)) — element-specific offer; Bridgwood v. A.J. Wood Constr. (480 Mass. 349 (2018)) — specialty-match requirement for expert; Matsuyama v. Birnbaum (452 Mass. 1 (2008)) — loss of chance. Structure: standard of care / breach / causation / damages / conclusion, with pinpoint records cites + supporting expert affidavit attached. Distinct from NJ AOM (§ 2A:53A-27), IL § 2-622, GA § 9-11-9.1, PA Rule 1042.3 — those are certificate-of-merit affidavits filed contemporaneously with the complaint, while MA tribunal is a post-answer screening body. 8–20 pages plus attachments.

verificationSources.citation: Cooper v. Cooper-Ciccarelli, 77 Mass. App. Ct. 86 (2010)
Bridgwood v. A.J. Wood Constr., Inc., 480 Mass. 349 (2018)

knownLimitations: Specialty-match expert requirement under Bridgwood v. A.J. Wood Constr. (480 Mass. 349 (2018)) — verify expert CV against defendant specialty before filing. Mismatch is the single most common reason for tribunal findings of insufficiency.
Offer of proof must be supported by an expert affidavit / letter containing the substance of the testimony; bare attorney-prepared narratives are routinely found insufficient.
Tribunal finding of insufficiency requires plaintiff to post $6,000 statutory bond within 30 days; failure to post results in dismissal. Plan accordingly with the client.
Tribunal proceedings are NOT a substitute for substantive litigation — the offer screens evidentiary sufficiency, not legal merit. Adversarial argument is unhelpful.
The offer is not admissible at trial under § 60B — it serves a screening function only. Tribunal findings are admissible at trial only under specific statutory conditions.

Loss-of-chance claims under Matsuyama v. Birnbaum (452 Mass. 1 (2008)) require additional record support — plaintiff must establish (i) reduced chance of better outcome, (ii) breach was substantial contributing factor in the reduction, (iii) percentage chance reduction.
Some tribunal decisions have rejected sufficient offers based on perceived inadequacy of records-pinpoint cites — pinpoint cites by date and page are essential.

Tribunal scheduling and panel composition vary by county — coordinate with the tribunal coordinator and the defendant before submission. Some panels prefer expert testimony in person; others accept expert affidavit only.
Discovery is generally NOT available pre-tribunal — pre-suit medical records collection and pre-suit expert retention are essential to a sufficient offer.
Statute of limitations is NOT tolled by tribunal — file the complaint within the M.G.L. c. 260 § 4 statute of limitations and proceed to tribunal thereafter.
